config.title: Berlin Airports - FAQ
config.subtitle: A local's plain answers on flying into Berlin in 2026: which airport, what happened to Tegel and Schönefeld, which terminal you arrive at, and how to reach the city.

items.q: How many airports does Berlin have in 2026?
items.a: One airport handles Berlin's scheduled passenger flights: Berlin Brandenburg Airport, code BER. Berlin's passenger air traffic was concentrated at BER after Tegel handled its final flight on 8 November 2020. Visitors now choose between terminals inside BER. Tegel and Tempelhof are former airports, while Schönefeld's former passenger terminal is the permanently closed BER Terminal 5.

No. Tegel (TXL) handled its final flight on 8 November 2020, after BER Terminal 1 opened. Its famous hexagonal terminal still stands, but the site is being redeveloped into the Urban Tech Republic and Schumacher Quartier. There are no passenger flights at Tegel, so if a friend or an old article points you there, redirect to BER.

Schönefeld (SXF) was not demolished; it was absorbed into BER. The old terminal became BER Terminal 5, which is now permanently closed. Schönefeld still exists as a place and an S-Bahn station, but it is not a separate passenger airport. If an itinerary still sends you to SXF or T5, check it with the airline.

items.q: Which terminal will I arrive at when I fly into BER?
items.a: Check your booking or BER's live flight information because airline assignments can change. Terminal 1 (T1) is the main building and currently includes Lufthansa and easyJet. Terminal 2 (T2) is smaller and currently includes Ryanair and Wizz Air. The signed walk between T1 and T2 takes about five minutes. The airport station is under T1 on level U2, reached from T2 through the level E0 passage. Terminal 5 is closed.

items.q: Can you still visit Tempelhof airport?
items.a: Yes, just not by plane. Tempelhof Airport (THF) closed in 2008, and Tempelhofer Feld opened to the public as a free park in 2010. Berliners cycle, skate and picnic on the former airfield, especially in summer, with barbecues limited to designated areas. The old terminal building also hosts events and tours.

items.q: How do I get from BER airport to central Berlin?
items.a: The FEX Airport Express runs about every 15 minutes through most of the day and reaches Berlin Hauptbahnhof in around 23 minutes via Südkreuz and Potsdamer Platz. Current regional lines are RE20, RB22, RB24 and RB32. The S9 and S85 each run every 20 minutes; the S9 is useful for Alexanderplatz and the Stadtbahn, but continuous overnight service applies only on Friday and Saturday nights. A taxi usually takes 35 to 45 minutes and costs roughly 55 to 65 euros for central Berlin; ride-hailing apps show their own live price. The airport station is under Terminal 1 on level U2; follow the signed passage from T2.

items.q: What ticket do I need for the train from BER airport?
items.a: BER is in fare zone C, so you need a Berlin ABC ticket for any train to or from the airport. In 2026 an ABC single costs 5.00 euros and is valid for 120 minutes in one direction, including transfers. Buy it before you board, then validate a paper ticket on the platform or activate a digital ticket before starting the journey.
